coredata.dc:title: Surface recognition strategy via ascorbic acid-triggered decomposition of boron nitride-loaded cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiaolin
Long, Chenggang
Tan, Yaozong
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: As for the accurate determination of analytes, most of chemical probes suffer from poor selectivity and data fluctuation. Sensor systems via colorimetric and fluorometric modes have caused considerable interests since the two ways are free from employment of special instrumentation and complicated techniques. A new type of cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheet has been developed and boron nitride is incorporated. Such nanoplatform can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OxOPD) with emission change. In the presence of ascorbic acid, CoOOH can be reduced to Co2+ ions and the generation of OxOPD has been suppressed. Therefore, the fluorescent signal of boron nitride has been recovered and ratiometric determination for ascorbic acid can be realized (detection limit: 1.07 μM; linear range: 0.05–80 μM). In addition, colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be transformed into oxidized form-TMBox with blue color and the sensitivity can reach as low as 120 nM. Current study will not only provide a typical case of colorimetric and fluorometric sensing for quantitative analysis of ascorbic acid, but also promote a new molecular design concept in practical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Visual detection
Ratiometric
Colorimetry
Dual-mode assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Boron nitride (BN) quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of AA based on CoOOH nanosheets
Colorimetric detection of AA based on CoOOH nanosheets
Detection principle of AA by dual-mode assay based on CoOOH nanosheets
Condition optimization for the ratiometric determination and colorimetry dual-mode assay
Analytical performance of dual-mode assay toward AA
Selectivity of the dual-mode assay toward AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (D) FT-IR spectrum and (D) XRD spectrum of the synthesized CoOOH nanosheets.
(A) TEM images, (B) diameter distribution of the synthesized boron nitride (BN) QDs.
TEM image (A) and of EDS(B) of BN/CoOOH nanohybrid.
Fluorescence spectra (λex = 360 nm) of BN/CoOOH nanohybrid (100 μL/mL) in the presence and absence of ascorbic acid (80 μM).

(A) Absorption spectra of CoOOH, TMB and CoOOH + TMB, (B) Absorption spectra of TMB, TMB + CoOOH, TMB + AA and mixture of AA and CoOOH + TMB. Sensing condition: CoOOH (30 μg/mL); TMB: 0.2 mM, AA: 18 μM, pH = 4.0.

(A) Fluorescence spectra against various concentrations of ascorbic acid with excitation of 360 nm. (Inset: Photographs of the BN/CoOOH) in aqueous solution under exposure to different concentrations of CoOOH nanosheets under 365 nm UV light. (B) A linear relationship of F452/F578 against the ascorbic acid concentration over the range from 0.05–80 μM. Sensing condition: BN/CoOOH nanohybrid, 100 μL/mL; OPD, 1.5 mM; pH = 7.0.

(A) Absorption spectra of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine solution (0.2 mM) upon addition of CoOOH nanosheets (30.0 μg mL−1) and ascorbic acid with different concentrations. (B) Linear relationship between (A0-A) and the concentrations of ascorbic acid (0.5–22 μM). Inset: Linear relationship in the concentration range from 0.5 μM to 22 μM for ascorbic acid. A and A0 are the absorbance in the presence and absence of ascorbic acid. The error bars represent the confidence interval for the mean of three measurements. (inset; Digital images of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine solution (0.2 mM) upon addition of CoOOH nanosheets (30.0 μg mL−1) and ascorbic acid with different concentrations).

(A) Selectivity of the assay for ascorbic acid compared to other potential interferences. The concentration of ascorbic acid = 80 μM, metal ions = 400 μM, other interferences = 80 μM, N-methylmaleimide = 240 μM. F456 refers to the fluorescence intensity of the boron nitride QDs; F578 refers to the fluorescence intensity of the OxOPD).) respectively. The error bars represent the confidence interval for the mean of three measurements. (B) Selectivity of the assay for ascorbic acid compared to other potential interferences. The concentration of AA = 22 μM, other interferences = 50 μM, A and A0 are the absorbance in the presence and absence of ascorbic acid, respectively. The error bars represent the confidence interval for the mean of three measurements.
Illustration of fluorometric and colorimetric assay of ascorbic acid by CoOOH nanosheets.

head.ce:title: Surface recognition strategy via ascorbic acid-triggered decomposition of boron nitride-loaded cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As for the accurate determination of analytes, most of chemical probes suffer from poor selectivity and data fluctuation. Sensor systems via colorimetric and fluorometric modes have caused considerable interests since the two ways are free from employment of special instrumentation and complicated techniques. A new type of cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheet has been developed and boron nitride is incorporated. Such nanoplatform can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OxOPD) with emission change. In the presence of ascorbic acid, CoOOH can be reduced to Co2+ ions and the generation of OxOPD has been suppressed. Therefore, the fluorescent signal of boron nitride has been recovered and ratiometric determination for ascorbic acid can be realized (detection limit: 1.07 μM; linear range: 0.05–80 μM). In addition, colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be transformed into oxidized form-TMBox with blue color and the sensitivity can reach as low as 120 nM. Current study will not only provide a typical case of colorimetric and fluorometric sensing for quantitative analysis of ascorbic acid, but also promote a new molecular design concept in practical field.

ce:section.ce:para: The newly developed nanoplatforms to integrate light harvest or photocatalysis systems have been paid much attention in terms of their extended surface area, various surface states and delicate microstructures [1–7]. During the formation of nanoscale materials, elemental doping would be an effective method to control the electronic structure and band gap [8]. In recent years, boron nitride (abbreviated as BN) as a 2D network from hexagonal structure has generated extensive interests [9，10]. When its particle size was decreased within 10 nm range, the quantum dots might induce the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and possess electrostatic attraction to holes because of oxygen-containing moieties with negative charges. Current studies reported its potential application in photocatalysis, energy storage, adsorption and thermal conductivity [11]. But the reported data for its photoluminescence property was very rare, especially the employment of its light response to external species has never been explored.

Cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheet would be another type of 2D layered nanomaterial and it has been investigated owing to excellent physical and chemical properties [8–11]. CoOOH nanoflakes can disperse in water due to rich hydroxyl groups on the surface. The nanomaterials possess wide absorption band and such spectral feature makes them as the potential energy acceptors to quench various fluorescent substances via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [12]. In the presence of antioxidant substances such as cysteine and ascorbic acid (AA), CoOOH nanosheet has been transformed into Co2+ ions and the absorbance has been suppressed. The effects of fluorescence resonance energy transfer are less important and the fluorescence intensity can be recovered [13–15]. Nevertheless, the development of CoOOH related sensor has just been started and most of dynamic systems were established on a single responsive process that would be prone to be disturbed by a variety of factors including background and environmental signals [16–18]. It can be well accepted that the ratiometric fluorescence can eliminate the external interference due to its self-correction capability. Such optical method can provide distinguished color changes for the purpose of visualization. Thus, it would be significant to develop the ratiometric determination method for analyte sensing [19–21].

As far as the guest species were concerned, ascorbic acid (AA), named as vitamin C, is a crucial neurochemical reagent in cerebral system and it maintains the body’s normal activities in many processes [22,23]. Meanwhile, it contains abundant unsaturated groups that are easy to be oxidized. Numerous studies supported the concentration of AA would be closely related to some diseases. For example, insufficient intake of AA can lead to fatigue, depression and scurvy, while higher concentration of AA may cause trouble for sleeping, stomach cramps and diarrhea [24]. Therefore, it would be valuable to find a sensitive, accurate and effective way for the determination of AA. At present, different strategies have been reported, including capillary electrophoresis [25], electrochemical methods [26,27] and mass connected to liquid chromatography [28,29]. However, the above analytical ways suffer from complicated treatment for matrix and electrode calibration. An inexpensive and efficient method like fluorescent detection can be considered as an alternative strategy for the sample analysis.

In this system, CoOOH nanosheet can directly oxidize the colorless 3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxidation product (TMBox) under acid environment in the absence of any oxidizing agent (e.g. H2O2), which is in a different way from other peroxidase-like nanomaterials [30,31]. Moreover, TMB is more sensitive than the traditional substrates such as 2, 2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6- sulfonic acid) (ABTS) [32]. Such TMB-incorporating process requires short reaction time and simple operation. It can be identified by eye observation and ultrahigh detectability can be achieved.

Based on the above discussion, a ratiometric method derived from fluorometric and colorimetric dual modes has been developed for ascorbic acid sensing. As illustrated in Scheme 1, Boron nitride quantum dots were quenched in the presence of CoOOH nanosheets due to FRET effect. During the process, the non-fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized into orange emissive oxidation product (2,3-diaminophenazine oxOPD). Upon the addition of AA, CoOOH nanosheets were reduced into Co2+ ions and the oxidase like activity could not be maintained. As a result, the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (oxOPD) did not occur and fluorescence resonance energy transfer was restricted. The experimental results revealed the fluorescent signal of oxOPD was vanished and the emission from boron nitride was recovered. In this way, the luminescence was evolved from orange to blue. In terms of the ratiometric detection, ascorbic acid in the range of 0.05–80 μM could be quantitatively monitored. In a totally different manner, the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by CoOOH nanosheets was switched off in the presence of ascorbic acid, the less generation of TMBox induced the decrease of blue color in the sample solution, as illustrated in route (b) of Scheme 1. The ascorbic acid within the range of 0.5–22 μM could be determined by this colorimetric method. Accordingly, the employment of oxidase-like activity from CoOOH nanosheets could offer two independent sensing routes for ascorbic acid. The combination of ratiometric measurement and colorimetric determination enlarged the detection range over 0.05–80 μM and decreased the detection limit down to 120 nM. The detailed information for the preparation, physical property and quantitative sensing were described in the following sections.

Ascorbic acid (AA, 99.99%), NaClO (6% ~ 14%), NaOH (99.7%), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 98%), Dopamine (98%), Malic acid (98%), Citric acid (99.5%), 3,3´,5,5´-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98%), Glucose (98%), Alanine (98%), Cysteine (99%), N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 99%), Arginine (98%), Sarcosine (98%), Fructose (99%), Histidine (99%), Tyrosine (99%) and Glutathione (GSH, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin company. CoCl2.6H2O (99%) was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). NaCl (99.5%) and KCl (99.5%) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification. The reaction buffer used in this work was 0.1 M NaAc (pH = 4.0). The buffer solutions at different pH were prepared by 0.01 M Tris-HAc. All the other chemicals in this work were in the analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a computer-controlled F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured within the 4000–400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer, Prestingge-21, using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Japan Science Ultima IV X-Ray Powder Diffractometer. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained with a JEM-1400PLUS TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan) accompanied by an operating acceleration voltage of 120 kV. Element mapping images were revealed by a Phenom Pro X Desktop Scanning Electron Microscope (USA). The zeta potentials were measured by using a Malvern laser particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Particle size was evaluated with dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Instrument, UK). The pH values were determined with a digital pH meter PHS-3 C (INESA, Shanghai, China) calibrated at room temperature (23 ± 2 °C) with standard buffers of pH 4.01, 6.86 and 9.18.

CoOOH nanosheets were prepared according to previous work with some modifications. Briefly, a portion of cobalt chloride (CoCl2.6H2O) solution (50 mL, 10 mM) was mixed with NaOH solution (1.5 mL, 1.0 M). The mixture was treated by ultrasonic irradiation for 1 min and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (20 min). After that, the precipitate was re-dispersed into water (50 mL) and a portion of NaClO (2.5 mL, 0.9 M) was added, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. Then, the mixture was centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with water, the product yield = 14.8%. The sample was finally re-dispersed into 50 mL of sterilized water, which was the CoOOH nanosheets stock solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Boron nitride (BN) quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Boron nitride quantum dots were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, boric acid (0.1 g) and melamine (0.0339 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL). The mixture was transferred to a Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the reaction mixture was cooled down naturally to room temperature. Finally, boron nitride quantum dots dispersion was purified through a filter with a pore size of 0.22 µm and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 mins. In order to remove the residual small-molecule impurities, the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (Mw = 1000) for 72 h, the purified solution was freeze-dried to obtain powders for further use (product yield = 23.9%).

The BN/CoOOH nanohybrid was obtained through mixing a certain amount of boron nitride quantum dots and CoOOH nanosheets. In brief, 1 mL of CoOOH nanoflake solution (40 μg/mL) was added to boron nitride solution (1 mL, 70 μg/mL), and the mixture was stirred and then incubated for 5 min under ambient condition. After that, the BN/CoOOH nanohybrid was afforded (product yield = 12.7%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of AA based on CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As for ascorbic acid detection, a volume of BN/CoOOH nanohybrid solution (100 μL) and a portion of ascorbic acid solution (100 μL) at different concentrations were mixed with 700 μL of Tris-HAc buffer (0.01 M, pH = 7). The o-phenylenediamine solution (100 μL, 1.5 mM) was then added to the mixture and reacted for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All the measurements were performed three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of AA based on CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: During the process of quantitative measurement of ascorbic acid, 30.0 μg mL−1 CoOOH nanosheets were mixed with different concentrations of ascorbic acid. Then, 200 L of 0.2 mM 3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine was added to give a final volume of 1 mL. Finally, the above-prepared solution was incubated for 5 min and transferred for UV–vis measurements. All the absorption profiles were collected three times.

In the ratiometric fluorescence mode, ascorbic acid (80 μM) was replaced by the control substances (KCl, NaCl, Malic acid, Citric acid, Dopamine, Dlucose, Alanine, Cysteine, N-methylmaleimide, Sarcosine, Fructose, Histidine, Tyrosine and GSH) at the same concentration of 400 μM. The fluorescence changes at two emission peaks were compared with the case of ascorbic acid.

In the colorimetric mode, ascorbic acid (22 μM) was substituted by the control substances (KCl, NaCl, Malic acid, Citric acid, Dopamine, Dlucose, Alanine, Cysteine, N-methylmaleimide, Sarcosine, Fructose, Histidine, Tyrosine and GSH) at the same concentration of 50 μM. The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded and the absorbance change was observed.

The ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets were efficiently prepared via a supersonic-induced redox reaction between CoCl2.6 H2O and NaClO. The microstructure of the synthesized CoOOH nanosheets was studied by TEM. It can be observed that the layer-like structure was afforded and quasi-hexagonal shape was formed ( Fig. 1 A). The nanodomains provided large surface area for the full attachment of various substances such as o-phenylenediamine and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine. In the same system, it could contribute to the reaction of CoOOH with ascorbic acid to realize the analytical purpose. In Fig. 1B, the nanosheets gave rise to a wide UV–vis absorption band from 340 nm to 800 nm with the peak maximum at 445 nm. In FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1 C), it was found that the characteristic absorption bands were located at 3446 cm−1 (−OH), 1637 cm−1 (Co−O), and 578 cm−1 (Co−O2−), which were consistent with previous reports [33]. The XRD patterns were given in Fig. 1D. Diffraction peaks at 20.15°, 39.15°, and 50.45° matched well with the (003), (012) and (015), respectively (JCPDS: No. 73–0497) [34], indicating that Co2+ was converted into CoOOH as Co3+ by the oxidation reaction. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) spectrum revealed that Co and O existed in the nanomaterial and the elemental composition was collected in Table S1 (Fig. S1). All the above results supported the generation of the CoOOH nanosheets and the uniform nanostructure was formed.

It is possible to design multi-component material by the selection of boron nitride. As provided in Fig. 2 A and B, the as-formed BN quantum dots were regular with spherical shape and dispersed with a narrow diameter distribution range from 3.2 to 4.2 nm. The average size was around 3.6 nm. In Figs. S2A, a broad absorption band around 3445 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching mode of O-H and N-H groups [35]. The peak at 1380 cm−1 was assigned to B-N stretching vibration. The signal at 910–1100 cm−1 was ascribed to the N-B-O bond [36]. The optical properties were further explored. As given in Fig. S2B, an absorption peak at 214 nm in the UV − vis absorption spectrum can be monitored. Such quantum dots exhibited bright-blue emission under irradiation of UV light at 365 nm. Its excitation and emission wavelengths were measured to be 360 and 450 nm, respectively. The fluorescence band possessed no shifts based on different excitation wavelengths from 290 to 400 nm (Fig. S2C). The energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) confirmed that B and N existed in the as-prepared BN QDs (Fig. S2D). Such structural information supported the quantum dots could be achieved in the presence of hydrothermal treatment.

Previous studies reported that CoOOH nanoflakes are considered as favorable fluorescence quenchers and can suppress emissions of various materials (such as persistent luminescence nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots or carbon dots) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The interaction between CoOOH nanoflakes and BN QDs was investigated. Fig. S3A showed that the absorption profile of CoOOH nanoflakes was largely overlapped with the emission spectrum of BN QDs, which suggested the possibility of energy transfer. As given in Fig. S3B, the fluorescence of the BN QDs was suppressed progressively depending on the increase of CoOOH, indicating that efficient quenching capacity could be achieved. To confirm the feasibility of assembling BN/CoOOH nanohybrids, the zeta potentials of BN QDs, CoOOH and BN/CoOOH nanohybrids were measured in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S3 C, the zeta potentials of BN QDs and CoOOH were − 15.8 and + 6.5 mV, respectively. The value of BN/CoOOH nanohybrids was − 11 mV, which indicated that the negatively charged BN QDs could be electrostatically adsorbed on positively charged CoOOH. Additionally, in TEM image of BN/CoOOH nanohybrids ( Fig. 3 A), BN QDs were distributed onto the surface of CoOOH nanosheet. Furthermore, the elemental analysis of BN/CoOOH nanohybrids by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) was provided in Fig. 3B. Compared with BN QDs, the data supported the presence of B, N, O and Co elements. The elemental composition data verified the purity of BN/CoOOH nanostructure was relatively high (mass ratio for B, N, O and Co = 96.6%) (Table S2). From the preceding results, it is evident that boron nitride has been encapsulated into the host.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of AA by dual-mode assay based on CoOOH nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4, the band at 578 nm (F578) of the BN/CoOOH nanohybrid containing OPD was strong whereas the peak at 452 nm (F452) was weak in the absence of AA. However, due to the addition of AA, the signal at F578 was reduced remarkably whereas the peak at F452 was increased. The change of the two signals indicated that ratiometric fluorescent sensing of AA could be feasible. CoOOH nanosheets possess specific oxidizing ability in contrast to other two-dimensional materials [37]. Previous work demonstrated that CoOOH can be reduced to Co2+ ion by ascorbic acid via an efficient redox reaction, which would result in dissociation of CoOOH nanosheets [34]. The drastic change of absorption lines and DLS results of the CoOOH nanosheets with the addition of AA verified the reduction process and decomposition (Fig. S4). o-phenylenediamine (OPD), a traditional chromogenic substrate, can be catalytically oxidized to produce OxOPD (2,3-diaminophenazine). The resultant OxOPD solution emitted bright orange fluorescence and had an UV–vis absorption band around 452 nm [38]. Firstly, the critical role of CoOOH nanosheetes in the oxidation of OPD was investigated. In Fig. S5A and B, under the same reaction conditions, there were no effective signals in fluorescence and absorption for the sample of pure OPD, while the reaction mixture of CoOOH nanosheets and OPD afforded strong emission at 578 nm as well as an intensive absorption at 452 nm, which was similar to previous reports. All the experimental results above corroborated that OPD was dramatically oxidized by CoOOH nanosheets into OxOPD. However, the OPD was hardly oxidized in the presence of AA (Fig. S5). The BN/CoOOH hybrid containing OPD gave similar optical profiles at absorption band of 452 nm compared to the reaction mixture of OPD and CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. S5C). In order to verify the CoOOH nanosheets function in the process of OPD oxidation, we also tried to study whether Co2+ ions had a similar capacity. As exhibited in Fig. S5B, no obvious emission at 578 nm was found for the mixture of Co2+ ions and OPD.

Likewise, the oxidase-like potential of CoOOH nanosheets was demonstrated in the typical chromogenic reaction of TMB. In such system, the colorless CoOOH made the colorless TMB oxidized into blue products (TMBox) in acidic condition, with a sharp band at 653 nm ( Fig. 5 A). Moreover, the performance of AA detection was shown in Fig. 5B, Compared with individual TMB in NaAc buffer, an obvious absorption peak at 653 nm could be observed after the addition of CoOOH nanosheets, which indicated that TMB was oxidized to oxTMB. Nevertheless, the absorption intensity of the sensing system was decreased substantially, when ascorbic acid was mixed with CoOOH nanosheets before the treatment, and then 0.2 mM TMB was added into the mixture solution. It suggested that CoOOH nanosheets were dissolved by AA. The reduction in absorption bands was obtained and an obvious color change from dark blue to light blue was observed (inset of Fig. 5B). It has to be mentioned that Co2+ generated from AA reacting with CoOOH nanosheets had no influence on tetramethylbenzidine (Fig. S6A). All the results indicated that the proposed system could be employed for the detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization for the ratiometric determination and colorimetry dual-mode assay
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clarifying the chemical process, various parameters related to the sensing performance were investigated, including the synthesis procedure and the oxidation condition for TMB. To improve the detection sensitivity, the concentration of OPD, pH, temperature and incubation time were optimized. Different pH values may have impact on the oxidation of AA and OPD. It has been reported that ascorbic acid was oxidized more easily in the alkaline condition rather than the acidic condition [39]. Hence, we studied the emission changes (F452/F578) of this system in the pH range of 7.0–10.0. In Fig. S7A, the F452/F578 was the highest at pH = 7.0, in the following test, we selected pH = 7.0 as the experimental condition. Moreover, the effects of OPD concentration on the sensing system were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S7B, F452/F578 reached its maximum value when OPD was fixed at 1.5 mM and such concentration was employed. In Fig. S7C, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F452/F578) was improved at the beginning based on the temperature increase. Then the value was decreased slowly and finally kept stable. Therefore, 37 °C was chosen in this study. The incubation time was also optimized. From Fig. S7D, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced due to the extension of reaction time, then the value reached a plateau after 30 min. Thus, 30 min was used in this work as the optimum incubation time.

The experimental details for the colorimetric assay of CoOOH-TMB were also studied. In Fig. S8A, since the concentration of CoOOH was fixed, the strongest absorption signal was obtained when 0.2 mM of TMB was added. Therefore, such concentration was applied for ascorbic acid detection in the next step. The influence of CoOOH concentrations on the oxidation performance was also investigated. Tetramethylbenzidine at 0.2 mM was incubated in the presence of CoOOH at different concentrations and the absorbance spectra were recorded (Fig. S8B). The intensity was found to be highly dependent on the concentration of CoOOH nanosheets. In order to study the effect of pH on the oxidizability of CoOOH, tetramethylbenzidine and CoOOH nanoflakes were mixed at different pH, and the absorption peak at 653 nm was measured (Fig. S8C). The solution at pH = 4.0 was determined to provide the highest oxidized potential. Optimization of the reaction time of tetramethylbenzidine was also studied. A mixture of 0.2 mM tetramethylbenzidine and 30.0 μg mL−1 CoOOH nanoflakes was incubated at pH = 4.0 and the absorbance change at 653 nm was recorded as a function of time (0–20 min). In Fig. S8D, the absorption profiles were increased rapidly and equilibrium state was reached at 5 min. The results supported an efficient transformation of tetramethylbenzidine to oxidation product by CoOOH nanoflakes was realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual-mode assay toward AA
ce:section.ce:para: In the ratiometric mode of CoOOH-OPD-BNQDs, ascorbic acid could reduce CoOOH into Co2+ ions and therefore inhibit the formation of OxOPD, thus the probability for fluorescence resonance energy transfer was low. As confirmed in Fig. 6A, under the optimal conditions, the emissions of boron nitride (increase) and OxOPD (decrease) were responsive to the presence of ascorbic acid from 0 to 100 μM. Such dual-signal change realized the ratiometric assay. A good linear relationship between F452/F578 and the concentration of ascorbic acid was obtained in the range from 0.05–80 μM (Fig. 6B, R2 = 0.989). The emitted color was evolved from yellow to blue (inset of Fig. 6B), indicating that the sensing performance and the visualization effect were superior to the non-ratiometric assay. The detection limit for ascorbic acid was estimated to be 1.07 μM according to 3σ/slope (σ denotes the standard deviation of blank signals).

In the colorimetric mode of CoOOH-TMB, ascorbic acid could also lead to the reduction reaction for CoOOH and the oxidation of tetramethylbenzidine was suppressed. Under the optimal conditions, the generation of TMBox, given as the absorbance at 653 nm, was decreased due to the addition of ascorbic acid from 0 to 22 μM ( Fig. 7A). According to the correlation between absorption and concentration of ascorbic acid, it was observed the linear function was formed from 0.5–22 μM (Fig. 7B). It is evident that the solution color became weak step by step (inset of Fig. 7B). Based on the calculation of 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements and s is the slope of the linear calibration, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 120 nM, the LOD value is comparable to or even much lower than that of previous studies [23,33]. The combination of ratiometric detection and colorimetric modes expanded the quantitative range from 0.05 to 80 μM and lowered the LOD down to 120 nM. The proposed nano-platform with satisfactory sensing performance could meet the requirement of searching ascorbic acid within complicated environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the dual-mode assay toward AA
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the two spectroscopic modes was explored by comparing the changes of fluorescence intensity and absorbance with the related reducing substances, metal ions, amino acids and other common biomolecules. As can be seen in Fig. 8, cysteine and glutathione could cause the signal changes and they might interfere with the detection of ascorbic acid. However, the presence of cysteine and glutathione could be controlled by biothiol-scavenger (N-methylmaleimide), which could specifically react with the thiol group. Except the two biothiols, the addition of other components to the analysis system is not capable of causing apparent changes. Thus, such nanoprobe will enable the determination of bio-active species in multiple ways [40–43].

The exploration of ratiometric determination would be very valuable during the process of bioanalysis. In this study, boron nitride could serve as the signal moiety and CoOOH nanosheets would act as the recognition component. In the initial step, the emission of boron nitride was suppressed by CoOOH nanosheets. In the absence of ascorbic acid, the BN/CoOOH nanostructure was capable of oxidizing o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into oxidation product (OxOPD) and the strong emission band at 578 nm was afforded. Due to the addition of ascorbic acid (AA), an efficient redox reaction between reductant (AA) and CoOOH could cause decomposition of CoOOH nanosheets and the transformation from OPD to OxOPD was inhibited. Therefore, the blue peak (boron nitride) at 452 nm was recovered (detection limit: 1.07 μM; linear range: 0.05–80 μM). In a colorimetric way, the ascorbic acid-dependent signal change made the absorption assay possible. CoOOH nanosheets were reduced by ascorbic acid preferentially and the generation from 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine to TMBox was reduced substantially. In this way, the absorption band at 653 nm (TMBox) was decreased and the naked eye observation was realized (detection limit: 120 nM; linear range: 0.5–22 μM). The results revealed that the CoOOH nanosheets based dual-modal assay can be extended to monitor a variety of antioxidant-related targets under different conditions.
Xiaolin Yang: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Chenggang Long: Formal analysis, Resources. Yaozong Tan: Validation. Qianming Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. appreciates the support from Science and Technology Plan of Guangdong Province (No. 2020A0505100055) and Guangzhou Science and Technology Plan (No. 202002030325).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159625.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
